topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

The shared glance across the crowded breakroom was electric, a sudden, jarring collision of shared history. Sarah’s cheeks burned, a heat that had nothing to do with the lukewarm coffee. She quickly looked down at her laptop screen, pretending to be intensely focused on a spreadsheet, the rhythmic clicking of her mouse a desperate attempt to drown out the sudden, loud drumming in her chest. When Mark finally turned to her, his smile was easy, entirely unaware of the internal earthquake he had just triggered. "Hey, Sarah. Fancy seeing you here." The casualness of his tone felt like a spotlight, exposing some hidden flaw she hadn't intended to reveal. She mumbled a noncommittal "Hi," the sound thin and reedy, and focused intently on straightening a stray napkin, wishing the floor would simply swallow her whole.

[story 2]

I nearly dropped my mug. The sight of his face, so familiar yet suddenly charged with an unfamiliar recognition, made my hands tremble. It was impossible. After all these years, after the distance, how could this be? I avoided his gaze, focusing instead on the chipped paint of the windowsill. When he finally spoke, his voice carried a warmth that felt both comforting and deeply unsettling. "You look like you've seen a ghost," he remarked, leaning against the counter. I managed a tight, brittle laugh that caught in my throat. I gripped the edge of the counter so hard my knuckles went white, acutely aware of the silence stretching between us, thick with unspoken recollections of a shared, forgotten summer.

[story 3]

The realization hit me like a physical blow when I saw the faded photograph tucked into the shared office drawer—a grainy picture from a summer camp sign. My breath hitched, shallow and fast. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to vanish, to dissolve into the beige cubicle walls. When he approached, his expression shifted from friendly curiosity to something softer, laced with a quiet melancholy. "Remember Camp Whispering Pines?" he asked, his voice low. I stared fixedly at a water stain on the ceiling tile, my posture rigid. I wanted to retreat, to shrink away from the very air he breathed, feeling every muscle in my body tense in a silent plea for invisibility.
